## Key Points

- Board unanimously voted to request a formal elementary reconfiguration proposal for the March 2 budget workshop; Chair DeAngelis expressed strong personal support while emphasizing the board must ultimately make the decision
- Budget forums held the prior week drew ~100 participants over two nights; key community themes included student needs as top priority, commitment to equity, desire for transparency, long-term financial sustainability, and protecting school quality
- Over 51 data requests submitted by the public through the new online Q&A system, with the majority focused on elementary reconfiguration details
- Board unanimously endorsed the South Portland City Council's Resolution #5-25-26 regarding federal immigration enforcement and community safety, following emotional public comment about children afraid to attend school
- Multiple public speakers (teachers' union, ed tech union, parents) urged the board to provide clearer communication about the budget situation and more meaningful engagement beyond surveys
- Member Feller stated he would be comfortable with a tax increase exceeding 6% rather than making draconian cuts to close the $7M gap, and advocated exploring early retirement buyouts for 41 eligible staff (estimated $1M+ savings)
- Finance committee reported the district is on track for a deficit in the current fiscal year (FY26), not just FY27
- Community school strategy unanimously approved: the district officially became a community school district, with plans to expand from the middle school to the high school
- Pre-K update: 64 seats in district plus 16 at Chickadee partnership; joined CDS cohort three for four-year-old special education services starting July 2026; exploring Headstart classroom partnership
- Teachers' association president requested the board seek a precise tax-increase target from the city council and noted that union bargaining will be required for any reconfiguration plans
